channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Metroid Dread developer MercurySteam conducts layoffs
item.content:encoded:
Via MercurySteam/505 GamesVia MercurySteam/505 Games

Metroid Dread and Blades of Fire studio MercurySteam has announced it is laying off workers. In a post on the studio's Linkedin page, the studio notes that this is "something common" now within the industry, but "it is nonetheless a difficult and painful situation."

"We want to express our deepest respect, gratitude, and support for the people affected, whose talent, effort, and commitment have been an important part of the studio's history," reads the post.

"If your studio or company is looking for experienced developers, please feel free to contact us at [email protected]. We will do everything we can to help these professionals find new opportunities."

The number of affected workers was not stated.

The layoffs come after studio management was accused of harassment and forced crunch last fall. Blades of Fire launched last spring on current-gen consoles, and the game's Steam page still shows a launch date of May 14 (two days from now). 

Related:Sega is lowering the priority of games-as-a-service titles

The affected workers now join the growing ranks of game developers caught up in the deluge of recent layoffs in the industry.

About the Author

Editor-in-Chief, GameDeveloper.com

Danielle is the editor-in-chief of Game Developer, with previous editorial posts at Fanbyte, VICE, and Polygon. She’s also a lecturer in game design at the Berklee College of Music, and a hobbyist game developer in her spare time.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/metroid-dread-developer-mercurysteam-conducts-layoffs
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Sega is lowering the priority of games-as-a-service titles
item.content:encoded:
Sega has reviewed its strategic plans around games-as-a-service (GaaS), and it's set to lower the priority of free-to-play games moving forward.

The news comes from the financial results for the year ending March 31, 2026. In a slide named "review of the GaaS strategy," the company outlines that new free-to-play titles struggled in FY2026/3.

Specifically, Sonic Rumble Party had a "weak performance," and there was a delay in the launch of some titles, although Sega didn't disclose which. The company also lamented its failure to make more from its collaboration with Rovio, the mobile dev giant that it acquired back in 2023.

No more "Super Game" from Sega

In the same slide, Sega announced it's canceling its "Super Game." This refers to an initiative announced back in 2021, which was set to see Sega partnering with Microsoft to create games using the Azure cloud platform. 

The Super Game has come in and out of the public eye in recent years. In late 2022, Sega reaffirmed its commitment to the project, hoping to have it in the hands of players by March 2026. The company was projecting it would deliver lifetime sales of at least 100 billion yen (around $672 million at the time).

Related:Metroid Dread developer MercurySteam conducts layoffs

Then, in 2023, Sega reportedly cooled its interest in the blockchain game market after previously suggesting it might leverage the technology to create its Super Game.

Moving forward, Sega plans to review the positioning of its pillars for medium to long-term growth. In other words, it has lowered the priority of free-to-play games. Some of the division's development personnel (over 100 people) have been transferred to the full game development division, too, focusing on the mainstay IPs.

As for Rovio, Sega says the mobile company will continue the efforts toward a global GaaS, but will focus on its own restructuring first.

Rovio laid off 36 employees in its latest wave of redundancies, which took place in October of last year. According to the studio, this was due to Angry Birds Dream Blast "not performing as expected." Notably, Rovio also said it'd be making some changes in its management structure to reflect its role as a "non-listed company," as well as "tightening" relations with Sega.

Last year, Sega president and COO Shuji Utsumi said that game companies with "a good record tend to have a strong [global] GAAS business."

As for Sega's financial report, the company saw an overall net loss of 5.7 billion yen ($31.6 million). Operating income from its entertainment contents, which includes the video game division, declined from 40.8 billion yen ($259 million) to 32.4 billion yen ($205.5 million). This includes a $200 million impairment loss for Rovio in this third quarter.

Related:Report: Microsoft Israel's general manager leaves amid alleged unethical use of Azure

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/sega-is-lowering-the-priority-of-games-as-a-service-titles
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
